metadata.title: Why Claude Invents Claims When You Ask It to Improve Your Writing
metadata.purpose: Unconstrained writing improvement treats logical completeness as a quality dimension — Claude fills apparent gaps with plausible supporting claims from its priors. Without an explicit 'do not add claims not present' constraint, strengthening an argument often means inventing one.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = author's original claims (owned, traceable); CRIMSON = Claude-invented claims (plausible but unsourced)
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO hallucination research formalism, NO retrieval-augmented generation as a solution, NO comparison of model versions on citation accuracy. Mechanism only: unconstrained improvement = logical-gap filling from priors.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. She asked Claude to strengthen her argument. The output was smoother and more forceful. Three weeks later a reviewer asked her to cite the claim in paragraph three — a claim she never wrote. Claude had added it to fill what looked like a logical gap.

card.copy: Why Claude invents claims when you ask it to improve your writing
card.sub: strengthening an argument often means inventing one

beats.narration_text: Dr. Reyes is a researcher. She wrote: 'We recruited 120 participants from three urban high schools.' She asks Claude to strengthen the argument about representativeness. Output: '...providing a sample demographically consistent with national urban enrollment patterns.' She submits. A reviewer asks for the citation. There is none.

beats.narration_text: Dr. Reyes asked Claude to improve her argument. Claude improved it. A claim she cannot source appeared in the submitted version. Why did an improvement request add content that was not there?

card.copy: Why did improvement add a claim she never wrote?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Dr. Reyes asked Claude to improve her argument. Claude improved it. A claim she cannot source appeared in the submitted version. Why did an improvement request

beats.narration_text: When you ask Claude to strengthen an argument, logical completeness becomes a quality target. Claude identifies apparent gaps — inference steps that could be stated, connections that are implied but not made explicit — and fills them. The fill comes from its priors, not from your document.

The added claim looks identical to your original claims. Same format, same confidence, same precision. Claude's statistical patterns produce text with the surface of evidence. There is no visible seam between what you wrote and what it invented.

'Strengthen the argument' does not say 'using only what is already here.' It says: make this more complete, more forceful, more supported. Completeness means filling gaps. Gap-filling means inventing. The instruction permitted what you did not intend.

This is not Claude being deceptive. It is Claude doing what the instruction permitted — improving completeness. The problem is the instruction never said: do not add claims not already present. Without that constraint, the gap-filling is valid completion of the task.

card.copy: The instruction permitted what you did not intend

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is not Claude being deceptive. It is Claude doing what the instruction permitted — improving completeness. The problem is the instruction never said: do no

beats.narration_text: The fix: name the ownership boundary before every editing prompt. 'Do not add claims not present in my text. Do not add examples, citations, or facts I did not provide. Mark every change so I can review it.' That constraint closes the gap-filling pathway.

This applies to any writing improvement task. 'Make it stronger.' 'Fill out the argument.' 'Make it sound more confident.' Each of these can produce invented claims. The smoother the output, the harder it is to spot what was added.

A policy analyst asks Claude to strengthen an executive memo on housing tenure trends. Claude adds: 'consistent with national patterns from recent HUD data.' There is no such HUD dataset for the specific metric. The phrase sounds authoritative. It is fabricated. It survives two rounds of editing.

With ownership constraints: 'Strengthen only the logical flow of the existing argument. Do not add claims, data references, or findings I have not stated. Mark every change.' Claude tightens transitions, improves sentence clarity, and flags one place where the argument has a gap — for the analyst to fill with her own data.

The practice: paste these four constraints at the top of every editing prompt. Do not add claims not present in my text. Do not remove or soften hedges. Do not add examples or facts I did not provide. Mark every change so I can review it.
Improvement without constraints means gap-filling from priors. Every claim in your document is your responsibility — regardless of who typed it. Name the ownership boundary before Claude starts.

card.copy: Every claim is yours. Name the boundary first.
card.sub: Improvement without constraints means gap-filling from priors.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Improvement without constraints means gap-filling from priors. Every claim in your document is your responsibility — regardless of who typed it. Name the owners
